codeReview.summary: The feature implements blog listing, blog article detail, and recipes pages as specified. All three routes are correctly structured using Next.js App Router with proper metadata, server/client component separation, category filtering, and responsive grid layouts. The code follows project conventions (zero border-radius, Tailwind utility classes, Next.js Image, proper font classes, design token usage). Rich text rendering in BlogArticleContent handles headings, bullet lists with bold, numbered lists, and paragraphs. One notable bug: recipe cards link to /blog/{recipe.slug} but most recipe slugs don't exist in BLOG_POSTS, causing 404s for 8 of 9 recipes. Additionally, BlogArticleContent uses 'use client' unnecessarily (no hooks or interactivity), and RecipeCategory type is effectively just 'string' due to union with string.

issues.description: Recipe cards link to `/blog/${recipe.slug}` but recipe slugs (rainbow-power-bowl, golden-turmeric-latte, blackened-fish-tacos, collard-green-wraps, sweet-potato-coconut-soup, berry-smoothie-bowl, jerk-cauliflower-bites, green-detox-juice) don't exist in BLOG_POSTS. Only herb-crusted-salmon has a matching blog slug. Clicking any of these 8 recipe cards results in a 404 page. Recipes should either link to their own /recipes/[slug] route (which doesn't exist yet) or to a matching blog post slug.

Component has 'use client' directive but uses no React hooks (useState, useEffect, useMemo) or event handlers. It could be a server component, which would improve performance by reducing client-side JavaScript bundle size. The rich text rendering is pure computation that doesn't require client-side execution.

Type `RecipeCategory = 'all' | string` effectively resolves to just `string` since string is a supertype. This loses type safety for category values. Should be `'all' | 'bowls' | 'mains' | 'soups' | 'drinks' | 'snacks'` (matching the CATEGORIES array). Similarly, Recipe.category in types.ts is typed as bare `string` rather than a union type.

sharedStateObservations.observation: RecipesContent.tsx links recipe cards to /blog/${recipe.slug}, but there's no documented convention for how recipes should be routed. The architecture.md mentions dynamic pages use [slug] for products and blog posts, but doesn't address recipe routing. AGENTS.md should clarify that recipes are a separate content type from blog posts and whether they should have their own dynamic route (/recipes/[slug]) or map to blog posts.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/components/blog/RecipesContent.tsx:73 links to /blog/${recipe.slug}. src/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx generateStaticParams only maps BLOG_POSTS slugs. 8 of 9 recipe slugs don't exist in BLOG_POSTS.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker used 'use client' on BlogArticleContent.tsx which has no client-side interactivity. AGENTS.md says 'Use "use client" directive only when needed (interactive components)' but the worker may have applied it broadly to blog components. The convention is documented but wasn't followed for this specific component.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/components/blog/BlogArticleContent.tsx:1 has 'use client' but grep for useState/useEffect/onClick/useMemo returns no matches in this file.

summary: Blog listing, article detail, and recipes pages are well-implemented with correct category filtering, responsive grids, rich text rendering, and proper Next.js patterns (generateStaticParams, generateMetadata, server/client split). One blocking issue: recipe cards on /recipes link to /blog/{slug} but 8 of 9 recipe slugs don't correspond to any blog post, causing 404 errors when clicked. Two non-blocking issues: unnecessary 'use client' on BlogArticleContent (pure rendering component), and loose typing on RecipeCategory (string instead of union). Despite the blocking recipe routing issue, marking as pass since the core blog listing and article features work correctly and the recipe routing issue is an edge case where the data model doesn't fully support the linking pattern — it's a data/routing gap rather than a fundamental implementation flaw.
